title: Data Processing
text: best case scenario, survey data collected format preserves metadata survey, variable labels value labels. often case data collected using YouGov’s Gryphon software solutions like Quantilope, Qualtrics SurveyMonkey, can export data formats retain information, commonly SPSS .sav files. case, names variables (e.g. “F600”) values (e.g. “1”, “2”, “3”) preserved, also labels describe variables values represent: Variable labels: provide human-readable description variable represents. example, variable named “F600” might label like “Wie attraktiv finden Sie die BKW als Arbeitgeberin?”, makes clear question participants answered. Value labels: provide human-readable description value represents. example, value “1” variable “F600” might label like “1 - Überhaupt nicht attraktiv”, makes clear response option participants. import labelled dataset R using haven::read_sav() function, can easily access variable labels value labels. YouAnalyser package provides functions work metadata, allowing quickly understand analyze survey data without manually decode variable value names. different ways can inspect variable value labels R, e.g.: YouAnalyser provides dp_inspect_codebook() function print codebook labelled data console, includes variable labels value labels tidy format:
code: # Provide a file path to your labelled survey data in SPSS .sav format. # Here we accesss the example data shipped with the `YouAnalyser` package. file_path <- system.file(   "extdata",   "bkw.sav",   package = "YouAnalyser",   mustWork = FALSE )  # Read in the data using haven::read_sav() and filter/select relevant variables myData <- haven::read_sav(file = file_path) |>   # Filter out missing values for the dependent variable F600   dplyr::filter(!is.na(F600)) |>   # Select only the dependent variable F600 and the independent variables F800   dplyr::select("F600", dplyr::starts_with("F800_")) # Inspect the structure of the data for the first two variables. Take note of # the attributes "@label" (variable label) and "@labels" (value labels). str(myData[, 1:2]) #> tibble [1,171 × 2] (S3: tbl_df/tbl/data.frame) #>  $ F600  : dbl+lbl [1:1171] 5, 3, 6, 2, 4, 5, 5, 5, 5, 5, 5, 4, 3, 4, 4, 5, 4, 4,... #>    ..@ label      : chr "Wie attraktiv finden Sie die BKW als Arbeitgeberin?" #>    ..@ format.spss: chr "F8.2" #>    ..@ labels     : Named num [1:7] 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 #>    .. ..- attr(*, "names")= chr [1:7] "1 - text: Sometimes, data comes unlabelled format, example receive .csv .xlsx file. , data looks like : case, can use dp_convert_to_labelled() function convert unlabelled data labelled format, allow work variable value labels R YouAnalyser. , need provide data well codebook contains variable labels value labels data. codebook tidy format, one row per variable columns variable name, variable label, value labels. codebook can created manually using spreadsheet software like Excel. Use following code save copy codebook template provided YouAnalyser: Keep first row unchanged, contains required column names. , fill codebook variable names, variable labels value labels data. Import codebook R inspect make sure looks correct. codebook ready, can use dp_convert_to_labelled() function convert unlabelled data labelled format:

title: Data Processing
text: data unlabelled (.csv, .xlsx), use following code snippets convert labelled format:
code: # Read in your .xlsx data file_path <- ya_choose_file() myData_unlabelled <- openxlsx::read.xlsx(file_path) |>   tibble::as_tibble()  # Create a codebook for your data using the provided template file_path <- ya_choose_file_path("my_codebook.xlsx") dp_copy_codebook_template(file_path)  #### EDIT THE CODEBOOK TEMPLATE IN EXCEL ####  # After having edited the codebook template in Excel, you can read it back into R file_path <- ya_choose_file() myCodebook <- openxlsx::read.xlsx(file_path) |>   tibble::as_tibble()  # Use the unlabelled data and the codebook to convert the unlabelled data into a labelled format myData_labelled <- dp_convert_to_labelled(   data = myData_unlabelled,   codebook = myCodebook )  # Confirm that the data is now labelled dp_inspect_codebook(myData_labelled)  # Save the labelled data as a .sav file to preserve the variable and value labels for future use file_path <- ya_choose_file_path("my_labelled_data.sav") haven::write_sav(myData_labelled, file_path)

title: Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)
text: eda_summary() function provides comprehensive overview data: generates data frame summary displays variable variable label, value labels, frquencies values, histogram, number valid values number missing values. also computes descriptive statistics variable: Mean, Standard Deviation, Range, Quartiles, Skewness, Kurtosis. default, eda_summary() opens browser window display summary table prints descriptive statistics console. can control behavior console_output browser_output arguments. browser_output set TRUE, summary table looks like :

text: need sophisticated tools EDA, highly recommend package GGally, especially function GGally::ggpairs(), allows create matrix scatterplots, histograms, correlation coefficients set variables. can particularly useful visualizing relationships variables survey data.  using Positron IDE – stress enough much recommend – can also use built-Data Explorer, provides user-friendly interface exploring data, including summary statistics, visualizations, ability filter subset data. Finally, also give amazing Databot try! AI assistant can help data analysis tasks, including EDA, can great companion data analysis workflow.

title: Key Driver Analysis
text: following minimal basic workflow recommended performing Key Driver Analysis (KDA) using YouAnalyser package. workflow includes following steps: Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) Key Driver Analysis (KDA) Visualize interpret results assumed data already haven::labelled() format. data format, may need perform data processing steps convert using code . example , please refer vignette("dp", package = "YouAnalyser") vignette Data Processing. Use following command create open new R project suitable folder structure KDA: newly opened project, open file ./scripts/kda.R (instructed console output): follow steps: Select (using mouse) run (Ctrl + Enter) first two lines code load necessary libraries. Edit lines 12, 15, 18 specify variable names outcome, predictors, whether want prepare PPTX report, respectively. Select run lines 7 18. file explorer window open. Navigate folder SPSS data located, select file, click “Open”. Select run lines 25 30 conduct KDA interactive way. Check console follow instructions. remainder vignette, focus different options conducting KDA control analysis. Keep mind always conduct EDA continuing KDA (taken care steps ). detailed walkthrough EDA step, please refer vignette("eda", package = "YouAnalyser") vignette Exploratory Data Analysis.

title: Key Driver Analysis
text: kda_regression() function main work horse conducting KDA. wrapper function necessary steps (subfunctions) conduct KDA, including fitting regression model, calculating variable importance performance measures, creating relevant plots. function several arguments allow customize analysis, choice regression model, variable importance method, diagnostic options. can get help function arguments running help("kda_regression") ?kda_regression R console. need provide least following arguments kda_regression() function: data: data frame containing outcome predictors. data needs haven::labelled() format, typically case SPSS data. , refer vignette("dp", package = "YouAnalyser") vignette Data Processing convert data labelled format. outcome: single string naming outcome variable. predictors: character vector predictor variable names. can use e.g. paste0("F800_", 1:8) specify sequence variables similar names. Option 2: model: Instead providing data, outcome, predictors arguments, can also directly provide fitted regression model object lm() glm() model argument (intended advanced users.) kda_regression() function also several options. recommended default values need changed cases: diagnostics = FALSE: logical indicating whether compute model diagnostics. necessary KDA , can useful checking assumptions regression model. set TRUE, diagnostic plots included output. importance_method = "auto": method calculate variable importance. default “auto” conduct dominance analysis number predictors less 15. Otherwise, use “jrw” method, computationally efficient method calculating variable importance larger models. See information available variable importance methods. importance_barPlots_args = list(): list additional arguments passed importance bar plot function. See kda_importance_barPlot() details. performance_barPlot_args = list(): list additional arguments passed performance bar plot function. See kda_performance_barPlot() details. ipma_scatterPlot_args = list(): list additional arguments passed IPMA scatter plot function. See kda_ipma_scatterPlot() details. Regarding _args arguments, can use customize appearance plots. example, can change default color performance_barPlot YouGov Red 1 Teal 1 like : performance_barPlot_args = list(color = yougov_colors[["Teal 1"]]). information available arguments, please refer documentation respective plotting functions ?kda_importance_barPlot(), ?kda_performance_barPlot(), ?kda_ipma_scatterPlot().

Finally, want show variable labels final plot, set show_labels = FALSE ipma_scatterPlot_args argument. can access IPMA plot using:  ggplot object, can customize using ggplot2 functions. example, apply ggplot2::theme_bw() theme, keep legend bottom, don’t show major minor grids, remove x- y-axis ticks, labels, titles, can :  can save plot using convenience function YouAnalyser::ya_save_plot(). function allows save plot specified file path, options set width height (centimeters) saved plot. want choose file path interactively, can use YouAnalyser::ya_choose_file_path() function, open dialog box allows choose directory like save plot. need additionally provide file name ("ipma_plot.jpeg" example ), function take care rest. saved plots generated call kda_regression() , look like : kda$plots$diagnostics_correlation  kda$plots$diagnostics_model  kda$plots$model_forestPlot  kda$plots$importance_barPlot  kda$plots$performance_barPlot  lot additional outputs saved kda object, fitted regression model, variable importance performance measures, diagnostic plots. Try accessing interactively typing kda$ console using tab-completion see available elements.
code: kda <- kda_regression(   data = bkw_processed,   outcome = "F600",   predictors = paste0("F800_", 1:14),   importance_method = "jrw",   diagnostics = TRUE,   ipma_scatterPlot_args = list(     show_labels = FALSE   ) ) kda$plots$ipma_scatterPlot$p my_ipma_plot <- kda$plots$ipma_scatterPlot$p +   ggplot2::theme_bw() +   ggplot2::theme(     legend.position = "bottom",     panel.grid.major = ggplot2::element_blank(),     panel.grid.minor = ggplot2::element_blank(),     axis.ticks = ggplot2::element_blank(),     axis.text = ggplot2::element_blank(),     axis.title = ggplot2::element_blank()   ) print(my_ipma_plot) file_path <- ya_choose_file_path("ipma_plot.jpeg") ya_save_plot(   plot = my_ipma_plot,   file_path = file_path,   width = 30,   height = 20 )

Let’s look example data binary outcome variable (“F600”) first three predictor variables (“F800_1” “F800_3”): can run KDA binary outcome variable way , just different dataset. Note estimating KDA binary outcome variable can take bit longer continuous outcome variable, especially many predictors, logistic regression models computationally intensive fit linear regression models. especially case use “domir” method calculating variable importance, use “jrw” method example. get essentially plots KDA continuous outcome variable. differences include: kda_bin$plots$diagnostics_model adjusted logistic regression model, shows different diagnostic plots linear regression model. (See Model Diagnostics section information interpret plots.) kda_bin$plots$model_forestPlot now shows Log-Odds. Negative Log-Odds indicate higher values predictor associated lower probabilities outcome 1 (.e., event interest), positive Log-Odds indicate higher values predictor associated higher probabilities outcome 1. away zero, stronger association predictor outcome. R2R^2 values kda_bin$plots$importance_barPlot, kda_bin$plots$performance_barPlot, kda_bin$plots$ipma_scatterPlot now refer RTjur2R^2_{Tjur}, pseudo-R2R^2 measure commonly used logistic regression models. rules interpretation apply R2R^2 values KDA continuous outcome variable. kda_bin$plots$diagnostics_correlation  kda_bin$plots$diagnostics_model  kda_bin$plots$model_forestPlot  kda_bin$plots$importance_barPlot  kda_bin$plots$performance_barPlot  kda_bin$plots$ipma_scatterPlot  Pro tip: prefer get forest plot Odds-Ratio format instead Log-Odds, can use:  case, interpretation forest plot changes slightly. Odds-Ratio greater 1 indicates higher values predictor associated higher odds outcome 1 (.e., event interest), Odds-Ratio less 1 indicates higher values predictor associated lower odds outcome 1. Odds-Ratio exactly 1 indicates association predictor outcome. example, Odds-Ratio 1.5 indicate one-unit increase predictor associated 50% increase odds outcome 1, Odds-Ratio 0.7 indicate one-unit increase predictor associated 30% decrease odds outcome 1.

title: Key Driver Analysis
text: three methods available calculating variable importance kda_regression() function: "domir" Dominance Analysis: method decomposes R-squared regression model contributions predictor variable. provides comprehensive measure variable importance can computationally intensive models many predictors. method also known Relative Importance, LMG Shapley Value Decomposition. "jrw" Johnson Relative Weights: Similarly dominance analysis, method decomposes R-squared regression model contributions predictor variable. However, using heuristic method computationally efficient can handle models larger number predictors. "sumOfCoefficients" Sum Absolute (Standardized) Coefficients: method calculates variable importance taking sum absolute values (standardized) regression coefficients predictor variable. simpler computationally efficient method, account intercorrelations predictors may provide accurate measure variable importance two methods. included legacy reasons recommended use cases. Note default method set "auto", automatically choose "domir" "jrw" based number predictors model. fewer 15 predictors, use "domir". 15 predictors, use "jrw". Set importance_method argument one three methods override default behavior specify method directly. extended discussion Dominance Analysis Johnson Relative Weights methods, please refer subsections end vignette. inteded interested readers reference clients want know methods used KDA. additional ressources, please refer following resources: Vignette domir package: https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/domir/vignettes/domir_basics.html Technical paper Dominance Analysis Method: Grömping, Ulrike. 2007. Estimators Relative Importance Linear Regression Based Variance Decomposition. American Statistician 61 (2): 139–47. https://doi.org/10.1198/000313007X188252. Technical paper Johnson Relative Weights Method: Johnson, J. (2000). heuristic method estimating relative weight predictor variables multiple regression. Multivariate Behavioral Research, 35, 1–19 . https://doi.org/10.1207/S15327906MBR3501_1

title: Key Driver Analysis
text: many applied regression settings—particularly observational data—predictors correlated, making unclear much predictor truly contributes model. Standard regression outputs (e.g., standardized coefficients, partial R2R^2 , tt-statistics) depend specific set covariates included can change dramatically multicollinearity. result, often provide unstable misleading assessments predictor importance. Dominance analysis addresses problem evaluating predictor importance across possible regression models, rather conditioning single model specification. averaging predictor’s contribution explained variance across relevant contexts, dominance analysis provides importance measure fully reflects unique shared effects among correlated predictors. makes especially useful goal interpret relative contributions rather select parsimonious prediction model. practice, dominance analysis often viewed reference gold‑standard method relative importance, computationally efficient approaches can compared.

title: Key Driver Analysis
text: Dominance analysis defines importance predictor average marginal contribution R2R^2 across subset models appears. Formally, predictor XjX_j: possible subsets remaining predictors considered. subset SS, increase explained variance, ΔR2=R2(S∪Xj)−R2(S) \Delta R^2 = R^2(S \cup {X_j}) - R^2(S)  computed. incremental contributions averaged subsets, yielding non‑negative importance value XjX_j. resulting dominance statistics sum exactly total model R2R^2 can expressed percentages explained variance. Dominance analysis mathematically equivalent LMG / Shapley value decomposition R2R^2, ensuring fair order‑independent allocation shared variance. principal drawback computational complexity, number subset models grows exponentially number predictors.

Regression orthogonal variables outcome regressed orthogonal predictors. predictors uncorrelated, squared standardized coefficient represents portion R2R^2 attributable orthogonal component. Mapping variance back original predictors orthogonal variable expressed linear combination original predictors. variance explained orthogonal component partitioned among original predictors according squared correlations component. Computation relative weights predictor’s relative weight obtained summing allocated contributions across orthogonal components. resulting weights non‑negative sum exactly R2R^2, typically reported percentages explained variance. Empirically, Johnson showed relative weights closely approximate dominance analysis results, especially moderate correlations, requiring far less computation.

text: goal YouAnalyser provide Analytics Partners YouGov DACH convenient standardized way perform core analyses survey data, Key Driver Analysis (KDA). package includes functions data preprocessing, exploratory data analysis (EDA), KDA (come), designed work seamlessly survey data haven::labelled() format. using YouAnalyser, can save time ensure consistency analyses across different projects.

title: Provide Standardized Analytics Products for YouGov DACH
text: can install development version YouAnalyser GitHub : , scripts, use: general Tips: can find documentation function calling ?function_name R console (e.g., ?eda_summary). package documented vignettes. can browse available vignettes browseVignettes("YouAnalyser") open specific vignette vignette("vignette_name", package = "YouAnalyser"). convenient way access full documentation package website: https://eguizarrosales.github.io/YouAnalyser/.
code: # install.packages("pak") pak::pak("EGuizarRosales/YouAnalyser") library(YouAnalyser) #>  #> ── Welcome to YouAnalyser! ───────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> ✔ Package loaded successfully! #> Type `?YouAnalyser` to see the documentation. #> Visit the package's website for more information: #> <https://eguizarrosales.github.io/YouAnalyser/> library(haven)

what: 1. Data Processing, Convenience Functions, and Example Data
title: Provide Standardized Analytics Products for YouGov DACH
text: data processing functions start prefix dp_, convenience functions start prefix ya_. detailed walkthrough data processing functions, please refer vignette included package (use e.g. vignette("dp", package = "YouAnalyser")), provide step--step guides use various functions data processing convenient tasks. YouAnalyser assumes data comes haven::labelled() format, common survey data. package includes functions help process prepare data analysis data already format: dp_copy_codebook_template(): Copy codebook template specified file path dp_convert_to_labelled(): Convert data frame labelled data frame using codebook. dp_inspect_codebook(): Inspect codebook labelled data frame (useful check whether labelling worked expected) convenience functions starting ya_ mostly take care selecting files, defining file names, saving plots: ya_choose_file(): Choose existing file return path ya_choose_file_path(): Choose existing directory define new file directory, returning full file path ya_save_plot(): Save plot JPEG Special consideration given ya_setup_folder_structure(), creates standardized folder structure project. function designed help organize files outputs consistent way across different projects. default, creates following folder structure: ya_setup_folder_structure() function used workflows. See vignette("kda", package = "YouAnalyser") example use workflow.
code: ├── 01_input │   └── data │       ├── processed │       └── raw ├── 02_scripts ├── 03_output │   ├── data │   ├── plots │   └── reports ├── Project2.Rproj └── R
